core_claim: Employees adopt unsanctioned AI tools faster than security can inventory them, so company data flows out through tools with no logging, retention, or review, and you cannot govern what you cannot see.

caption:
Most AI risk in companies right now is not a sci-fi attack. It is shadow AI, the tools your team adopted before anyone reviewed them.

Microsoft's 2024 Work Trend Index found that 78 percent of people using AI at work bring their own tools, often unsanctioned. IBM's 2025 Cost of a Data Breach report found that one in five breached organizations traced a breach to shadow AI, that it added about $670,000 to the average breach cost, and that most breached organizations still had no AI governance in place. OWASP lists sensitive information disclosure as a top risk for LLM apps.

The problem is visibility, not malice. When a tool is not sanctioned, there is usually no logging, no retention policy, and no data-handling review behind it, so data flows out through paths security cannot see. The team often finds out only after the data has already left.

Defender move: start with visibility, not bans. Inventory which AI tools are actually in use, using your proxy, SSO, and browser telemetry. Offer a sanctioned alternative with clear data rules so people do not route around you. Then run every AI tool through the same vendor and security review as any other software.

You cannot govern the AI you cannot see. Inventory first, then write the policy.

Do you have a current list of the AI tools your team actually uses, and how would you even build it?

Follow for AI security breakdowns without the fake panic.
comment_prompt: Do you have a current, real list of the AI tools your team uses, and how would you even build it?

Context slide: a vast dark warehouse of stacked crates, most bare and unlabelled, only a few near the front carrying a paper tag.
Risk slide: a single lit lantern marking one charted path while several unlit trails branch off into shadow.
Mechanism slide: an open manifest on a desk with most lines filled but a run of blank rows in the middle catching a thin rim of light.
Failure point slide: a ship already small on the dark horizon with a fresh wake, seen from an empty watchtower in the foreground.
Defense slide: a cartographer's hand drawing a fresh boundary onto a dark map, scattered markers now circled and labelled inside it.
Takeaway slide: a single bright pin dropped onto a dark unmarked map, a faint grid resolving into focus around it.
CTA slide: a single clipboard holding a blank checklist resting in vast dark negative space, with the comment question and handle.

sources.source: Microsoft & LinkedIn 2024 Work Trend Index: AI at Work
sources.supports: 78% of AI users at work bring their own AI tools (BYOAI), often without IT sanction.

sources.source: IBM Cost of a Data Breach 2025 (newsroom)
sources.supports: One in five breached orgs traced a breach to shadow AI; shadow AI added ~$670K to average breach cost; most breached orgs lacked AI governance.

sources.source: OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications (2025): LLM02 Sensitive Information Disclosure
sources.supports: Sensitive information disclosure via LLM apps is a top risk; mitigations include data-handling policy and restricting what enters the model.

narration.text: The AI your team uses the most is probably the AI you never approved.
Microsoft found that 78 percent of AI users at work bring their own tools, usually before anyone reviews them.
So start with visibility, not bans. Inventory what is actually in use, offer a sanctioned option, and run each tool through the same vendor review as any other software.
You cannot govern the AI you cannot see. Inventory first, then write the policy.
Do you have a current list of the AI tools your team actually uses? Tell me below.
